coredata.dc:title: GPx-mimetic selenium-enriched yeast nanozymes ameliorate diabetic bone disease via dual-targeting of ROS scavenging and angiogenesis-osteogenesis coupling

coredata.dc:creator: Wu, Zimei
Hou, Qiaodan
Qin, Lang
Chen, Tingting
Yang, Kunkun
Wei, Fuxin
Wang, Lin
coredata.dc:description:
Diabetic bone disease (DBD) is a severe skeletal complication arising from metabolic dysregulation and redox imbalance during diabetes progression. Its core pathological mechanism involves reactive oxygen species (ROS)-mediated decoupling of angiogenesis-osteogenesis, yet no targeted therapies exist. Herein, we present a biosynthesis strategy to engineer selenium-doped carbon quantum dots (SeYCQDs) from selenium-enriched yeast (SeY) as a bifunctional nanozyme for DBD treatment. By leveraging the bioconversion process of SeY, inorganic selenium is biotransformed into organoselenium metabolites, followed by hydrothermal synthesis to fabricate SeYCQDs with glutathione peroxidase (GPx)-mimetic activity. Mechanistically, under diabetic conditions, SeYCQDs (1) repair mitochondrial membrane potential in vascular endothelial cells (VECs) through GPx-catalyzed ROS scavenging, thereby restoring endothelial function, and (2) activate the VEGF/BMP2/Noggin signaling axis to promote type H vessel (CD31
+
EMCN
+ ) neovascularization and osteoblast differentiation, thereby sustaining angiogenesis-osteogenesis coupling. This study establishes the first yeast-based nanozyme synchronizing antioxidant defense with metabolic coupling repair, providing a clinically translatable paradigm for diabetes-associated osteometabolic disorders.

(a2) Medium magnification showing the nanoparticles. (a3) High-resolution TEM image of SeYCQDs. (B) EDS elemental mapping of SeYCQDs:(b1) SEM image of SeYCQDs. (b2) Carbon (C) distribution. (b3) Oxygen (O) distribution. (b4) Nitrogen (N) distribution. (b5) Selenium (Se) distribution. (b6) Elemental mass percentages of SeYCQDs, showing C + O + N (95.4 %) and Se (4.6 %). (C) AFM characterization of SeYCQDs: (c1) 2D AFM topography image. (c2) 3D AFM reconstruction of SeYCQDs. (c3) Line-scan analysis of SeYCQDs showing particle height distribution. (D) XPS analysis: (d1) Full survey spectrum of SeYCQDs, and and high-resolution spectra of (d2) C 1s (d3) N 1s (d4) O 1s (d5) Se 3d. (E) UV–Vis absorption spectrum and photograph of SeYCQDs under blue light (BL) and UV light (UV-L). (F) Fluorescence emission and excitation spectra of SeYCQDs. (G) Assessment of the O2 •− scavenging efficiency of SeYCQDs at different concentrations. (H) ESR spectra analysis of the •OH scavenging by SeYCQDs. (I) Proposed antioxidant mechanism of SeYCQDs. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

Biocompatibility and cellular uptake of SeYCQDs. (A) CCK-8 assay shows the cytotoxicity of different concentrations of SeYCQDs (0.01–1 mg/mL) in bEnd.3 (endothelial cells) and MC3T3-E1 (osteoblast precursor cells) (n = 5). (B) Under diabetic conditions, CCK-8 assay demonstrates the proliferation of bEnd.3 and MC3T3-E1 cells treated with SeYCQDs (0.05 and 0.1 mg/mL) for 4 days (n = 8). (C) Confocal fluorescence microscopy images illustrate SeYCQDs uptake and localization in bEnd.3 and MC3T3-E1 cells (cyan fluorescence); bright field (BF) images highlight cell morphology and integrity. (D–E) Quantitative fluorescence intensity profiles indicate that SeYCQDs are predominantly localized around the nucleus in bEnd.3 (D) and MC3T3-E1 (E) cells. All data are expressed as mean ± SD. Statistical significance was determined using one-way ANOVA with Tukey’s post hoc test. ∗p < 0.05, ∗∗p < 0.01, ∗∗∗p < 0.001, ∗∗∗∗p < 0.0001. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

Impact of SeYCQDs on cell behavior under diabetic conditions. (A) EdU staining showing the proliferative activity of bEnd.3 (endothelial cells) and MC3T3-E1 (osteoblast precursor cells) under normal medium (NM), diabetic medium (DM), DM with NAC (DM_NAC), and DM with SeYCQDs (DM_SeYCQD). (B–C) Quantification of EdU staining intensity, reflecting the proliferative volume (μm3) of bEnd.3 (B) and MC3T3-E1 (C) cells. (D) Immunofluorescence staining of actin cytoskeleton (phalloidin, red) and focal adhesion protein vinculin (VCL, green) in bEnd.3 and MC3T3-E1 cells. (E–F) Quantification of VCL volume (μm3), indicating focal adhesion levels in bEnd.3 (E) and MC3T3-E1 (F) cells. All data are expressed as mean ± SD. Statistical significance was determined using one-way ANOVA with Tukey’s post hoc test. ∗p < 0.05, ∗∗p < 0.01, ∗∗∗p < 0.001, ∗∗∗∗p < 0.0001. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

Evaluation of the osteogenic activity of SeYCQDs. (A) qPCR analysis of mRNA expression levels of osteogenesis-related genes (n = 3). (B)The represent images of Runx2 stained. (C) Quantitative analysis of Runx2 staining results (n = 4). (D) Histochemical staining image of ALP activity. (E) Quantitative analysis of ALP activity (n = 4). (F) Representative images of ARS staining. (G) Quantitative analysis of ARS (n = 7). All data are expressed as mean ± SD. Statistical significance was determined using one-way ANOVA with Tukey’s post hoc test. ∗p < 0.05, ∗∗p < 0.01, ∗∗∗p < 0.001, ∗∗∗∗p < 0.0001.

Assessment of the angiogenic activity of SeYCQDs. (A) Representative wound healing images of endothelial cells (bEnd.3) at 0, 6, and 24 h under normal medium (NM), diabetic medium (DM), and diabetic medium treated with SeYCQDs (DM_SeYCQD). The white dashed lines indicate the wound edges. Scale bar: 200 μm. (B) Quantitative analysis of wound area (n = 6). (C) Quantitative analysis of wound healing rate at 6 and 20 h (n = 6). (D) Representative tubule formation images of bEnd.3 cells under NM, DM, and DM_SeYCQD. Scale bar: 100 μm. (E) Quantitative analysis of the total number of junctions (n = 3). (F) Quantitative analysis of total vessel length (n = 3). (G–I) qPCR analysis of angiogenesis-related genes (Vegf, Pdgfa, and Fgf1) in a co-culture system of endothelial cells (bEnd.3) and osteoblasts (MC3T3-E1) (n = 3). (J–L) qPCR analysis of angiogenesis-osteogenesis coupling genes (Noggin, Bmp2, and Wnt3a) in the co-culture system (n = 3). All data are expressed as mean ± SD. Statistical significance was determined using one-way ANOVA with Tukey's post hoc test. ∗p < 0.05, ∗∗p < 0.01, ∗∗∗p < 0.001, ∗∗∗∗p < 0.0001.

Transcriptomic analysis and validation of the regulatory effects of SeYCQDs under diabetic conditions. (A–C) GO enrichment analysis of DEGs in biological processes (BP), cellular components (CC), and molecular functions (MF). (D) Significant GO terms related to oxidative stress, adhesion, extracellular structure, and antioxidant activity. (E) KEGG pathway analysis highlighting ECM-receptor interaction, NF-κB signaling, and apoptotic pathways. (F–G) GSEA enrichment of apoptosis (F) and oxidative stress signaling pathways (G). (H–J) Heatmaps of DEGs associated with vascular formation (H), adhesion and migration (I), and antioxidant/inflammation-related processes (J). (K) PPI network of core DEGs involved in vascular formation, migration, and antioxidant responses. (L) Heatmap of core DEGs selected for qPCR validation. (M − O) qPCR validation of vascular formation-related (Vegfd, Fbln2), antioxidant-related (Sod3, Nqo1, Gsta3), and inflammation-related (Ccl2, Cxcl2, Il34) genes. All data in bar charts (M − O) are presented as mean ± SD. Statistical significance: ∗p < 0.05, ∗∗p < 0.01, ∗∗∗p < 0.001, ∗∗∗∗p < 0.0001.

Validation of the antioxidant and cytoprotective effects of SeYCQDs under diabetic conditions. (A) Detection of ROS levels using DCF-DA fluorescence probes in NM, DM, and DM_SeYCQD groups. (B) Quantitative analysis of DCF-DA fluorescence intensity (n = 8). (C–D) Measurement of MDA levels and GSH concentrations in cells (n = 6). (E–I) qPCR analysis of antioxidant-related genes (Gpx4, SOD1-3, CAT, SIRT3 and Nrf2) in different groups (n = 3). (J–K) Flow cytometric analysis of mitochondrial membrane potential (ΔΨm) using JC-1 staining and quantification of green fluorescence representing depolarized mitochondria (n = 3). (L–M) Flow cytometric analysis of apoptosis using Annexin V/PI staining and quantification of early apoptotic cells (n = 3). All data are expressed as mean ± SD. Statistical significance was determined using one-way ANOVA with Tukey's post hoc test. ∗p < 0.05, ∗∗p < 0.01, ∗∗∗p < 0.001, ∗∗∗∗p < 0.0001. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

+
EMCN
+ , magenta and yellow) and Osx
+ osteoblasts (green) in NM, DM, and DM_SeYCQD groups. Surface rendering demonstrates spatial colocalization of type H vessels and Osx+ osteoblasts. (C–D) Quantitative analysis of type H vessel volume (C) and Osx
+ osteoblast volume (D) (n = 7). (E) qPCR analysis of angiogenesis-osteogenesis coupling-related gene expression, including Noggin, Vegf, Fgf1, Igfb1, Pdgfa, Bmp2, and Wnt3a (n = 3). Effects of SeYCQDs on new bone formation in vivo under diabetic conditions. (A) Timeline of the T1D mouse model and sampling at day 28 post-treatment. (H–M) Quantitative analysis of cortical bone parameters, including cortical bone mineral density (cTMD; H), bone surface/volume ratio (BS/BV; I), cortical thichness (Ct.Th; J), porosity (PO; K), moment of Inertia (MMI; L), and bone surface area (BAr; M) (n = 5). (P–Q) Von Kossa (VG) staining of bone sections showing mineralized bone, with quantitative analysis of mineralized bone percentage (Q) (n = 5). All data are expressed as mean ± SD. Statistical significance was determined using one-way ANOVA with Tukey's post hoc test. ∗p < 0.05, ∗∗p < 0.01, ∗∗∗p < 0.001, ∗∗∗∗p < 0.0001.

Histopathological and Hematological Assessment of SeYCQDs in Diabetic Mice. (A) Histopathological analysis of major organs (heart, liver, spleen, lung, and kidney) in diabetic mice treated with either vehicle or SeYCQDs. H&E staining was performed at magnifications of 4 × and 20 × . The scale bars represent 100 μm (4 × ) and 50 μm (20 × ). (B–E) Hematological analysis of blood samples from diabetic mice treated with vehicle or SeYCQDs. (C) Red blood cell (RBC) count, hemoglobin (HBG), mean corpuscular hemoglobin (MCH), and mean corpuscular volume (MCV). (D) Mean corpuscular hemoglobin concentration (MCHC) and hematocrit (HCT). (E) Platelet (PLT) count, mean platelet volume (MPV), platelet distribution width (PDW), and plateletcrit (PCT). ∗Data are represented as mean ± SD. Statistically significant differences were determined by one-way ANOVA with Tukey's multiple comparison test. ns, not significant; ∗∗P < 0.01, ∗∗∗P < 0.001, ∗∗∗P < 0.0001. head.ce:title: GPx-mimetic selenium-enriched yeast nanozymes ameliorate diabetic bone disease via dual-targeting of ROS scavenging and angiogenesis-osteogenesis coupling

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para:
Diabetic bone disease (DBD) is a severe skeletal complication arising from metabolic dysregulation and redox imbalance during diabetes progression. Its core pathological mechanism involves reactive oxygen species (ROS)-mediated decoupling of angiogenesis-osteogenesis, yet no targeted therapies exist. Herein, we present a biosynthesis strategy to engineer selenium-doped carbon quantum dots (SeYCQDs) from selenium-enriched yeast (SeY) as a bifunctional nanozyme for DBD treatment. By leveraging the bioconversion process of SeY, inorganic selenium is biotransformed into organoselenium metabolites, followed by hydrothermal synthesis to fabricate SeYCQDs with glutathione peroxidase (GPx)-mimetic activity. Mechanistically, under diabetic conditions, SeYCQDs (1) repair mitochondrial membrane potential in vascular endothelial cells (VECs) through GPx-catalyzed ROS scavenging, thereby restoring endothelial function, and (2) activate the VEGF/BMP2/Noggin signaling axis to promote type H vessel (CD31
+
EMCN
+ ) neovascularization and osteoblast differentiation, thereby sustaining angiogenesis-osteogenesis coupling. This study establishes the first yeast-based nanozyme synchronizing antioxidant defense with metabolic coupling repair, providing a clinically translatable paradigm for diabetes-associated osteometabolic disorders.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Globally, there are over 537 million people suffering from diabetes mellitus (DM), and projections suggest that this number will increase to 783 million by 2045 [1]. Among its complications, diabetic bone disease (DBD) is often overlooked but poses significant challenges. [2–4], characterized by an increased risk of fragility fractures [5–7], delayed fracture healing or failure [7], high fracture recurrence rates [8], etc. Despite advancements in blood glucose management, no targeted therapies for DBD are currently available, and existing interventions fail to address its underlying pathological mechanisms: hyperglycemia-induced vascular-bone metabolic decoupling [9]. Alarmingly, certain antidiabetic medications, such as insulin, thiazolidinediones, and sulfonylureas, exacerbate bone metabolism dysfunction [10], further underscoring the urgent need for new treatment strategies.

Diabetic vasculopathy, driven by chronic hyperglycemia and insulin resistance, initiates systemic microangiopathy that disrupts the "angiogenesis-osteogenesis" axis [9,11–15]. Type H vessels, a specialized bone vascular subtype, regulate osteogenesis through dual mechanisms: (i) nutrient/oxygen supply (e.g., hypoxia-regulated VEGF secretion), and (ii) paracrine signaling (Noggin, BMP2) to activate osteoprogenitors [11–13]. However, our previous work revealed that diabetic conditions reduce type H vessel density significantly via ROS-mediated endothelial dysfunction, leading to spatial-temporal mismatch between vascular remodeling and bone formation [16–20]. These findings position vascular preservation as a linchpin strategy for DBD management. Under diabetic conditions, oxidative stress arises not only from excessive reactive oxygen species (ROS) generation but is also closely linked to the depletion of antioxidant enzymatic activity, particularly that of glutathione peroxidase (GPx) [21–23]. Reduced GPx expression directly impairs osteogenic function and disrupts bone metabolic homeostasis [24,25]. These findings highlight a novel therapeutic paradigm for diabetic bone disease (DBD): enhancing selenium-dependent GPx activity to restore vascular-bone metabolic crosstalk may serve as a breakthrough strategy for mitigating DBD.

Selenium (Se), an essential trace element in humans, serves as the catalytic core of GPx through the formation of selenocysteine (Sec), which confers peroxide-reducing capacity to the enzyme and sustains cellular redox homeostasis [26]. Selenium deficiency is associated with growth retardation (e.g., delayed osteogenesis) [27], whereas its optimal supplementation enhances insulin sensitivity [28] and demonstrates therapeutic potential in diabetic complications such as non-alcoholic fatty liver disease (NAFLD) and obesity [29]. However, despite its benefits, selenium toxicity remains a significant concern. Excessive selenium intake can result in selenosis, which causes symptoms cytoxicity, genotoxicity, and carcinogenesis [30,31]. Therefore, achieving a balance between selenium bioavailability and toxicity remains a critical challenge for clinical translation. Carbon quantum dots (CQDs), owing to their tunable physicochemical properties and low systemic toxicity, have emerged as an ideal platform for selenium delivery [32–34]. Selenium-doped CQDs (SeCQDs) mimic the chain-breaking antioxidant activity of GPx by stabilizing radical intermediates via robust Se-C bonds, demonstrating therapeutic efficacy in diverse disease models, including myocardial infarction [35], β-cell protection [36], and acute kidney injury [37]. Recent studies have also explored CQDs in the context of diabetes-related diseases. For example, antioxidant-functionalized CQDs have shown efficacy in reducing oxidative stress [38] and improving insulin resistance [39], while others have been applied for diabetic wound healing [40]. These studies highlight the emerging role of CQDs in managing diabetic complications. However, few existing systems combine the redox regulatory capacity of selenium with the structural advantages of CQDs. Compared with conventional CQDs, selenium-containing nanozymes—especially those capable of GPx-mimicry and endogenous pathway activation—offer greater potential for long-term therapeutic outcomes. Nevertheless, existing SeCQD synthesis strategies predominantly rely on elemental selenium (low bioavailability) [41], organoselenium compounds (synthetic complexity) [42], or metallic selenides (heavy metal toxicity) [43,44], which significantly hinder their biomedical applicability.

Selenium-enriched yeast (SeY), a natural and sustainable source of bioavailable selenium, was utilized in this study to synthesize selenium-doped carbon quantum dots (SeYCQDs) through a green biomaterial-driven approach [45]. Leveraging the bioconversion processof SeY, inorganic selenium was bioconverted into organoselenium compounds (e.g., selenocysteine and selenomethionine) [46], followed by hydrothermal carbonization to construct SeYCQDs (Scheme 1 ). Mechanistically, SeYCQDs exhibited GPx-mimetic activity, effectively scavenging ROS and restoring mitochondrial function in vascular endothelial cells (VECs). This intervention further upregulated the expression of VEGF, BMP2, and Noggin, thereby reconstructing the angiogenesis-osteogenesis coupling critical for bone regeneration. The yeast-derived SeYCQDs demonstrated enhanced biocompatibility and clinical translatability due to their biosynthesis strategy. SeYCQDs operate through a dual-targeting mechanism: (i) mitigating oxidative stress via ROS neutralization and (ii) reinstating vascular-bone metabolic crosstalk by reactivating angiogenic-osteogenic signaling pathways. To our knowledge, this study represents the first report of a yeast-based selenium nanozyme with dual therapeutic axes specifically engineered for DBD.

The inorganic selenium species in SeY were biotransformed into organoselenium metabolites (e.g., selenocysteine and selenomethionine) through the endogenous transsulfuration pathway of yeast [47,48]Synthesizes selenium-doped carbon quantum dots (SeYCQDs) by employing Selenium-enriched yeast via a hydrothermal method [49]. Specifically, 50 mg of selenium-enriched yeast was dissolved in 25 mL ultrapure water, and the PH was adjusted to 10 to enhance solubility. The solution was stirred at room temperature for 2 h and then heated at 120 °C for 12 h in the isothermal reactor. Centrifuged The resultant mixture at 12 000 rpm for 15 min; finally, the supernatant was dialyzed and freeze-dried to yield SeYCQDs as a brown powder.

The instruments involved are listed as follows. Transmission Electron Microscopy (TEM): Tecnai G2 F30 electron microscope was employed to capture high-resolution images. Energy Dispersive X-ray Spectroscopy (EDS): Elemental composition and mapping were analyzed using an Oxford X-Max 80 detector attached to the Tecnai G2 F30 TEM, operated at 200 kV. X-ray Diffraction (XRD): Recorded using a Rigaku Smartlab 9 KW. Atomic Force Microscopy (AFM): Images obtained in tapping mode with the MultiMode 8 system. Dynamic Light Scattering (DLS): Particle zeta potential was measured using a NANO ZS ZEN 3600 instrument. UV/Vis Spectroscopy: Absorption spectra were collected using a Lambda 25 spectrophotometer. Time-Correlated Single Photon Counting (TCSPC): Fluorescence lifetimes measured with a Nanolog-TCSPC (Horiba JY, Japan) at 405 nm excitation. X-ray Photoelectron Spectroscopy (XPS): Spectra obtained using a PHI 5000 Versaprobe III after freeze-drying. Immunofluorescence Microscopy: Conducted with a Zeiss LSM980 confocal laser scanning microscope.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Scavenging assay of O2•− and •OH by SeYCQDs
ce:section.ce:para:
The scavenging abilities of SeYCQDs against superoxide anion (O2•−) and hydroxyl radicals (•OH) were evaluated using two distinct assays. For O2•−, the scavenging efficiency was determined by measuring the reduction of NBT (Nitroblue tetrazolium) in the presence of SeYCQDs. Various concentrations of SeYCQDs (0.01, 0.1, 0.5, 1 mg/mL) were added to the reaction mixture containing NBT, and the absorbance was measured at a wavelength of 560 nm to determine the O2•− scavenging efficiency. The scavenging efficiency was calculated as:
Scavenging
Efficiency
( % )
=
Absorbance
of
Control − Absorbance
of
Sample
Absorbance
of
Control
× 100 %

For •OH scavenging, hydroxyl radicals were generated using the Fenton reaction with the Fe2+/H2O2 system. SeYCQDs (0.1, 1 mg/mL) were added to the generated •OH radical solution. After the addition of 5,5-Dimethyl-1-pyrroline N-oxide (DMPO), ESR spectra were recorded to assess the •OH scavenging ability. The changes in the ESR signal intensity upon the addition of SeYCQDs indicated the effective scavenging of •OH radicals.

Mouse brain microvascular endothelial cells (bEnd.3) and mouse embryonic osteoblast precursor cells (MC3T3-E1) were cultured in Dulbecco's Modified Eagle's Medium (DMEM) supplemented with 10 % fetal bovine serum (FBS) and 1 % penicillin/streptomycin (referred to as "NM") and incubated at 37 °C in a 5 % CO2 environment. Each cells at the third passage were seeded at 1 × 104 cells/mL in 24-well plates and cultured for 24 h before switching to a diabetes-induced medium, which medium system containing extra 25 mM glucose [50] (referred to as "DM"), refreshed every two days. For MC3T3-E1, the medium was altered to an osteogenic differentiation medium (OriCell-RAXMD-90021). SeYCQDs (0.1 mg/mL) was added to the DM medium, referred to as " DM_SeYCQDs".

Cell viability and cytotoxicity were evaluated through the Cell Counting Kit-8 (CCK-8) test. bEnd.3 and MC3T3-E1 cells were regularly seeded with 2 × 103 cells per well in 96-well plates and incubated at 37 °C with 5 % CO2 for 12 h. A range of SeYCQDs concentrations (0, 0.01, 0.05, 0.1, 0.5, and 1 mg/mL) were applied to intervente cells. After 24 h, the medium was replaced with 100 μL of CCK-8 mixture: DMEM mixture (1:10 vol ratio) and incubated at 37 °C with 5 % CO2 for 1–4 h. Absorbance was measured at 450 nm using a Bio-Tek ELx800 microplate reader. Viability was expressed as a percentage relative to control. Additionally, both cells were treated with 0.5 mg/mL SeYCQDs, and their viability was monitored at 1, 4, and 6 days to assess drug effects under diabetic conditions using the same CCK8 method.

bEnd.3 and MC3T3-E1 cells were cultured with 1 × 105 cells at 35 mm confocal dishes in DM medium and incubated at 37 °C with 5 % CO2 for 12 h, then treated with 0.5 mg/mL SeYCQDs. After 12 h, cells were fixed with 4 % paraformaldehyde (PFA) and imaged using the LSM 710 confocal microscope (Carl Zeiss, USA) at 40 × magnification.

Cell culture and SeYCQDs intervention was described above.Cell proliferation was determined using an EdU Cell Proliferation Imaging Kit (Abbkine, Cat# KTA2031). Fixed and stained cells following with the manufacturer's instructions, EdU incorporation and nuclear staining were visualized under fluorescence microscopy at excitation/emission wavelengths of 546/565 nm and 360/460 nm, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Immunofluorescence staining for cell morphology and adhesion
ce:section.ce:para: Cell culture and SeYCQDs intervention were described above. Post-intervention cells were fixed with 4 % paraformaldehyde (PFA) for 15 min at room temperature, followed by permeabilization with 0.1 % Triton X-100 for 10 min. After blocking with 5 % bovine serum albumin (BSA) for 1 h, cells were incubated overnight at 4 °C with primary antibodies against vinculin (1:200). Following PBS washes, cells were treated with Alexa Fluor 488-conjugated goat anti-rabbit IgG (1:500) and Alexa Fluor 594-conjugated phalloidin (1:1000) for 1 h at 37 °C in the dark. Nuclei were counterstained with DAPI (1 μg/mL) for 5 min. Fluorescent images were captured using the LSM980 confocal microscope (Zeiss) at 40 × magnification with triple-channel detection. Cell spreading area and focal adhesion counts were quantified using ImageJ software (NIH) with Focal Adhesion Analysis Plugin.

Cell culture and SeYCQDs intervention were described above. Cell migration was assessed using CellTracker Green (Abbkine, Cat# KTA1002) to label the live cells. These cells were then seeded at 2 × 106 cells per well in 48-well plates and cultured until confluence. After the cells were serum-starved for 2 h, a 10 μL pipette tip was employed to make a straight wound. The cells were washed with PBS and then incubated in serum-free medium at 37 °C with 5 % CO2. Wound closure was imaged at 0, 6, 12, and 24 h post-wounding using the LSM980 microscope (Zeiss). Migration distances were quantitatively analyzed using imageJ software.

The tube formation assay was conducted to assess the angiogenic capability of endothelial cells. Briefly, a pre-chilled 96-well plate was coated with 50 μL of Matrigel (Corning, Cat# 356234) and incubated at 37 °C for 30 min to solidify the Matrigel. Endothelial cells (e.g., bEnd.3) were collected, suspended in complete culture medium, and seeded onto the Matrigel-coated wells at a density of 1 × 104 cells per well. After incubation at 37 °C for 6–8 h, the tube-like structures formed by endothelial cells were observed under an inverted microscope. Images were taken, and the total tube length, the number of branch points, and the number of loops were quantified using ImageJ software (NIH, USA) with the Angiogenesis Analyzer plugin. All experiments were performed in triplicate to guarantee reproducibility. The results were presented as quantitative values to indicate the tube-forming ability of endothelial cells under different treatment conditions.

The culture of MC3T3-E1 cells and the intervention of SeYCQDs were described above. MC3T3-E1 cells cultured in osteogenic medium (OriCell-RAXMD-90021) and intervented with SeYCQDs were fixed and stained for ALP activity on day 14 with a color development kit (Biosharp, Cat# BL709A). ALP activity was quantified by measuring the absorbance of the supernatant at 405 nm using an assay kit (Beyotime Biotechnology, China).

To assess osteogenic differentiation, cells were stained with a 2 % ARS solution post 21 days of culture in osteogenic medium (OriCell-RAXMD-90021). After fixation and rinsing, cells were stained, washed, and then treated with 10 % acetic acid. The absorbance of the neutralized supernatant was measured at 405 nm for quantification.

Total RNA was isolated by the triazole regent method combined with ribosomal RNA depleted, and strand-specific adapters were added to fragmented RNA. The quality of cDNA libraries was confirmed before sequencing at Maiwei Metabolism (China). Data were analyzed with TopHat2 to assess alignment, and differential mRNA expression was determined using |log2FoldChange| > 1 threshold and a q-value <0.001.

Principal component analysis (PCA) assessed replicate correlation and differential gene expression. Differential expression was analyzed by applying Audics software. Genes were annotated against the Gene Ontology (GO) database, and pathway analysis was performed using the Kyoto Encyclopedia of Genes and Genomes (KEGG).

Total RNA from bEnd.3 cells, MC3T3-E1 cells, and bone tissue was extracted using RNA-easy™ Isolation Reagent kit (Vazyme, Cat# RC112-01). cDNA was synthesized with PrimeScript™ RT Master Mix kit (Vazyme, Cat #R323-01). RT-qPCR was conducted using 100 ng of cDNA, specific primers, and ChamQ SYBR Color qPCR Master Mix (Vazyme, Cat# Q712). Gene expression was normalized to Gapdh. The primers sequence listed were in Table S1.

The intracellular ROS levels were assessed using 2′,7′-dichlorofluorescein (DCF) fluorescence probe. bEND.3 cells were incubated in DM-induced medium at 37 °C with 5 % CO2 and treated with fresh medium containing SeYCQDs compounds for 48 h. After cells covered the bottom of the culture dish, cells were treated with the DCFH-DA (Sigma, 10 μM) and observed under the LSM980 confocal microscope (Zeiss).

Intracellular MDA concentrations were assessed using a Cellular Lipid Peroxidation MDA Assay Kit (Abbkin, Cat# KTB1050). After the indicated treatments, mineralized osteocytes were harvested and lysed in RIPA lysis solution. The cell lysate was centrifuged at 12 000×g for 5 min, and the supernatant was collected for subsequent experiments. The MDA measurement procedure strictly followed the manufacturers’ instructions.

A GSH assay (Abbkine, Cat # KTB1600) was performed according to the kit protocol to evaluate the impact of the scaffolds on GSH activity. The bEnd.3 cells were rinsed with cold PBS twice and resuspended the extraction buffer. The cells underwent two cycles of freeze-thawing, alternately in liquid nitrogen and a hot water bath (37 °C). After 10 min centrifuged (4 °C), supernatant was obtained. The provided processing steps were adhered to for the treatment of both samples and standards, and the absorbance was gauged at 412 nm utilizing a microplate reader.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of apoptosis by flow cytometry
ce:section.ce:para: Cell apoptosis assays were assessed with an Annexin V-FITC/propidium iodide (PI) kit (Abbkin, Cat# KTA0002) according to the manufacturer's introduction. Briefly, cells were harvested after 3 days and washed with PBS. Then, the cells stained with FITC labeled Annexin V and propidium iodide for for 30 min. Flow cytometer (BD Immunocytometery Systems, San Jose, CA, USA) performed to detect cell apoptosis rate. Analyzed using FlowJo software (Tree Star).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of mitochondrial membrane potential by JC-1 assay
ce:section.ce:para: Mitochondrial membrane potential (Δψm) was assessed using the JC-1 Mitochondrial Membrane Potential Detection Kit (Abbkin, Cat# KTA4001) following the manufacturer's protocol. Briefly, after 3 days of treatment, cells were harvested and washed twice with PBS. The cells were then resuspended in 500 μL of JC-1 working solution and incubated at 37 °C for 20 min in the dark. After staining, the cells were washed twice with JC-1 staining buffer to remove excess dye. The mitochondrial membrane potential was detected using a flow cytometer (BD Immunocytometry Systems, San Jose, CA, USA). JC-1 aggregates, which indicate intact membrane potential, emit red fluorescence, while JC-1 monomers, which indicate depolarized membrane potential, emit green fluorescence. The percentage of cells with depolarized mitochondria was analyzed using Flow Jo software (Tree Star). Results were expressed as dot plots with red and green fluorescence intensities, and the mitochondrial depolarization rate was calculated based on the Q3 quadrant population.

All procedures were conducted in accordance with the animal care and use guidelines of the Southern University of Science and Technology (Approval No: SUSTC-JY202204012). Male C57BL/6 mice, 4 weeks old, were obtained from Jiangsu Jiyu Pharmaceutical Co., Ltd. The mice were housed in ventilated cages at a controlled temperature of 23 °C within a specific pathogen-free and accredited facility under a 12-h light/dark cycle. All assessments were performed using a blind experimental design.

The type 1 diabetes mellitus (DM) model was established in mice through intraperitoneal injections of streptozotocin (STZ, dissolved in 50 mM sodium citrate buffer-pH 4.5, 100 mg/kg, daily for 5 consecutive days, Cat# S0130, Sigma). For the administration of treatments, mice were anesthetized with isoflurane gas and restrained. Two months after successful establishment of DM, SeYCQDs (100 mg/mL, 10 μL) was injected into the bone marrow cavity of the femurs of DM mice using a 1 mL syringe with a 0.4 mm needle; the control mice received an equivalent volume of saline. Treatments were administered every three days [51]. Mice were euthanized at 14 and 28 days post-treatment initiation and tissue samples were carefully collected for subsequent analysis.

Fresh femurs were processed and cryo-embedded in a gelatin-based medium per the protocol described in reference [52]. Cryo-embedded femur samples were sectioned to 40 μm through microtome cryostat. Immunostaining utilized rabbit anti-Cd31, goat anti-endomucin (Emcn), and rabbit anti-Osterix (Osx) antibodies (1:50, 1:100, 1:200 respectively, Abcam/R&D Systems). After being washed with PBS for three times, sections were incubated with secondary antibodies to corresponding species conjugated with Alexa 488, 594 or 647 (Millipore; 1:500) at 25 °C for 1.5 h while avoiding light. The nuclei were counterstained with 40, 60-diamidino-2-phenylindole (DAPI, Sigma). LSM980 confocal microscope was used to take and collect pictures. Quantitative analysis was performed using Imaris software (Bitplane, v7.4.1).

Micro-CT scans were performed using the Skyscan 1276 system (Bruker) under blind conditions. Mice were anesthetized with 2 % isoflurane and positioned in a custom holder. Initial 2D scout scans targeted the femur's proximal metaphysis to define the analysis regions. Measurements included trabecular volume fraction (BV/TV), trabecular number (Tb.N), trabecular thickness (Tb.Th) and trabecular separation (Tb.Sp). The cortical parameters like cortical bone mineral density (cTMD), cortical thickness (Ct.Th), cortical porosity (Po(tot)), and surface/volume ratio (BS/BV). Structural changes were visualized with three-dimensional reconstructions via CTvox software.

Tissue samples were fixed in 4 % PFA, decalcified in 0.5 M EDTA (pH 7.2) for three weeks, and embedded in paraffin after dehydration in a graded ethanol series. Paraffin sections of 7 μm were stained with Hematoxylin and Eosin, Masson's trichrome, and Van Gieson (VG) for morphological assessments. Imaging was conducted using a standard light microscope with Image J software for digital analysis. Quantitative evaluations included bone volume and collagen fiber fraction, the latter being the ratio of bone tissue area to implant pore area. Statistical comparisons were made to delineate structural variances.

Statistical analyses Statistical comparisons between two groups were conducted using the Mann–Whitney U test (two-tailed) with GraphPad Prism 8.0 (GraphPad Software, USA). One-way ANOVA followed by Tukey's test was employed for statistical comparisons between three or more groups. All quantitative data are expressed as the mean ± SD. The p value < 0.05 was considered to be statistically signifcant.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Structural Characterization and Antioxidant Mechanism of SeYCQDs
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 1 A shows the transmission electron microscopy (TEM) image of SeYCQD. At low magnification (Fig. 1A–a1-a2), the nanoparticles exhibited monodisperse distribution with an average diameter of 3.2 ± 0.5 nm and no observable aggregation, confirming their colloidal stability. High-resolution TEM (HRTEM, Fig. 1A–a3) showed distinct lattice fringes with a spacing of 0.32 nm, corresponding to the (002) crystallographic of graphite carbon. This observation was further supported by the X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern (Fig. S1), which showed a prominent (002) diffraction peak at 26°, consistent with the sp2-hybridized carbon framework critical for photoluminescence and electron transport [53]. Atomic force microscopy (AFM) imaging (Fig. 1C) showed that the root mean square (RMS) roughness was measured as 0.65 ± 0.12 nm (Fig. 1C, c1); the 3D reconstruction map showed that the maximum vertical height change was 1.2 nm (Fig. 1C, c2), aligned with monolayer carbon quantum dot structures. Line-scan analysis (Fig. 1C, c3): Particle heights ranged from 1.0 to 1.8 nm, consistent with the lateral dimensions derived from TEM. Dynamic light scattering (DLS) measurements (Fig. S2) yielded a ζ-potential of −18 mV, suggesting moderate electrostatic repulsion between particles. Combined with the monodispersity data of TEM/AFM, this indicated that the colloidal behavior of SeYCQDs in aqueous media is stable, which is crucial for in vivo applications.

Energy dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDS) analysis (Fig. 1B) confirmed the homogeneous distribution of C (88.19 wt%), O (6.10 wt%), N (1.10 wt%), and Se (4.6 wt%) within the nanostructure. The C/Se atomic ratio was 126:1 (Fig. 1B, b6), which ensures the selenium loading without destroying the quantum confinement effect and balances the catalytic activity and structural integrity. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy results (XPS, Fig. 1D) indicated that the CQDs were mainly composed of carbon, nitrogen, oxygen, and selenium according to the typical peaks of C 1s, N 1s, O 1s, and Se 3d and corresponding content of these elements was inserted in Fig. 1D–d1. The deconvoluted XPS spectra of C 1s spectrum (Fig. 1D, d2): Dominated by sp2-hybridized carbon (C=C/C–C, 284.8 eV, 68.4 %) coexisting with oxygen- and nitrogen-containing groups (C–O/C–N, 286.03 eV, 24.1 %; O–C=O, 288.03 eV, 7.5 %). The deconvoluted N 1s spectrum (Fig. 1D, d3): pyridinic N (398.98 eV, 52 %), pyrrolic N (399.98 eV, 33 %), and oxidized N species (N–O, 400.97 eV, 15 %), which together enhance π-conjugation and electron transfer. The deconvoluted O 1s spectrum (Fig. 1D, d4): quinone/carboxyl groups (C=O, 531.49 eV, 61 %) and hydroxyl/epoxy functionalities (C–O, 532.8 eV, 39 %) functional groups with redox activity. The deconvoluted Se 3d spectrum (Fig. 1D, d5): Predominant Se–O bonding (59.66 eV, 80 %), and minor C–Se covalent interactions (55.94/56.74 eV, 20 %), indicating that the selenium configuration of surface oxidation was stabilized by yeast-derived oxygen species during hydrothermal synthesis. The higher bond energy of Se–O (∼523 kJ/mol) compared to C–Se (∼305 kJ/mol) ensured thermodynamic stability while enabling controlled selenite (SeO3 2−) release to sustain bioactivity [54]. This unique bonding configuration established dual active sites: (i) Se–O as Lewis acid centers to polarize reactive oxygen species (ROS). (ii) C–Se mimicking the catalytic triad of glutathione peroxidase (GPx).

The UV–Vis absorption spectrum (Fig. 1E) shows a prominent peak at 280 nm, corresponding to the π-π∗ transition of the aromatic sp2 domain in the graphite core [55]. Under UV irradiation, SeYCQDs emit strong blue fluorescence (Fig. 1E, inset), which is attributed to the fact that selenium-induced surface defects expose redox active sites. The photoluminescence (PL) spectra (Fig. 1F) showed that there were obvious excitation peaks and emission peaks at 350 nm and 450 nm, respectively, indicating that defect-mediated electron transitions are critical for ROS scavenging. Enzyme-mimetic assays revealed exceptional antioxidant efficiency: O2•− scavenging: 90 % efficiency at 1 mg/mL SeYCQDs (Fig. 1G). •OH neutralization: ESR spectra (Fig. 1H) showed that at a higher concentration of 1 mg/mL SeYCQDs, the •OH signal was nearly eliminated, indicating synergistic quenching via C=O electron donation and Se–O hydrogen abstraction [48,56–58]. The three-enzyme simulated cascade catalysis (Fig. 1I) supports these results.1.SOD-like activity: SeYCQDs converted O2 −• to H2O2 via Se–O-mediated electron transfer (2O2 −• + 2H+ → H2O2 + O2), which uses the conductivity of the graphite core to accelerate electron transfer. CAT-like activity: Graphitic carbon-enhanced H2O2 decomposition (2H2O2 → 2H2O + O2) through accelerated by conductive substrate-assisted charge transport, which minimizes secondary •OH generation through Fenton reaction. 3. •OH neutralization: The C=O group on the surface donates electrons to •OH, while Se-O extracts hydrogen to form a stable H2O. This cascade establishes a self-sustaining antioxidant cycle (O2•− → H2O2 → H2O/O2, which is dynamically regulated by the functional architecture of SeYCQDs. The first stage: acute scavenging of ROS through Se-O-dominated catalysis. The second stage: continuous defense through C-Se-mediated GPx-like cycle and SeO3 2− release, effectively disrupting oxidative cascades in diabetic pathologies.

The above characterization results reflect the four functional components of SeYCQDs: graphite core: providing quantum confinement effect for fluorescence stability and serving as an electron transport channel. Oxygen/nitrogen matrix: COOH/–NH2 functional groups enhance hydrophilicity and mediate bone matrix interaction. Se-O dominant surface: as the main ROS scavenging center, through acid-base catalysis. C-Se minority sites: mimic GPx catalytic triad (Se-C-N) for selenium-dependent enzyme regeneration. This structure allows SeYCQDs to achieve a two-stage therapeutic effect: (i) acute ROS neutralization through surface Se-O, and (ii) sustained antioxidant defense through the slow release of SeO3 2− and GPx-like cycles. The GPx-like activity of SeYCQDs is primarily driven by the selenium component, which mimics the catalytic triad of GPx. The Se–C bonds within SeYCQDs facilitate electron transfer, allowing them to neutralize ROS such as H2O2 and lipid peroxides efficiently. The Se–O and Se–C bonds enhance their redox activity, enabling SeYCQDs to act as electron donors, stabilizing radical intermediates in a manner similar to GPx. The multi-enzyme mimicry of SeYCQDs further extends their antioxidant capabilities, allowing them to interact with a wide range of ROS and protect cells from oxidative damage. This mechanism positions SeYCQDs as a powerful tool for mitigating oxidative stress, especially in the context of diabetes-related complications.

In addition to GPx-mimetic activity, SeYCQDs may enhance their therapeutic efficacy through other antioxidant mechanisms. The presence of Se–O and C=O functional groups enables direct quenching of hydroxyl radicals and superoxide anions. Furthermore, previous studies suggest that selenium-based nanomaterials can interact with intracellular antioxidant pathways. For example, Bin Xie et al. demonstrated that selenium nanoparticles could alleviate atopic dermatitis by activating selenoproteins through the Nrf2–Keap1 axis [59]. Similarly, XiaoNa Li et al. reported that BSA-stabilized selenium nanoparticles ameliorated intracerebral hemorrhage-like pathologies by inhibiting ferroptosis via activation of the Nrf2/G px 4 axis [60]. These properties suggest that SeYCQDs may establish a multifaceted redox defense system that not only mimics enzymatic functions but also reprograms cellular antioxidant networks, thereby further enhancing ROS-scavenging capacity.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Biocompatibility and cellular uptake of SeYCQDs
ce:section.ce:para: We first evaluated the cytotoxicity of SeYCQDs cin endothelial cells (bEnd.3) and osteoprogenitor cells (MC3T3-E1) at a concentration range: 0.01–1 mg/mL. As shown in Fig. 2A, SeYCQDs showed excellent biocompatibility in both cell lines, and no significant cytotoxicity observed even at the highest concentration (1 mg/mL). Notably, bEnd.3 cells showed a dose-dependent enhancement in viability at higher concentrations (0.5–1 mg/mL). Compared with the untreated control group, the survival rate increased by about 15 % (p < 0.01). This suggests that SeYCQDs may exert potential pro-angiogenic or metabolic stimulatory effects on endothelial cells, which may be mediated by selenium-dependent redox signaling pathways, such as PI3K/Akt or Nrf2 activation [61,62]. In contrast, no significant changes in viability were observed in MC3T3-E1 cells at any concentration tested (ns, p > 0.05), further confirming the excellent biocompatibility of SeYCQDs for bone-related applications [63,64]. The divergent responses between endothelial and osteoblast may reflect differences in selenium metabolism or receptor-mediated uptake mechanisms, which is worthy of further study of cell-specific signaling cascades.

Under high glucose conditions, SeYCQDs (0.05–0.1 mg/mL) significantly restored the proliferation of bEnd.3 and MC3T3-E1 cells (Fig. 2 B). Compared to the DM group, the cell viability of the SeYCQDs treatment group was increased by about 25 % (p < 0.001), which indicated that SeYCQDs could effectively promote cell proliferation in a diabetic environment. This may be due to the ability of SeYCQDs to scavenge ROS (such as O2•− and •OH), thereby alleviating oxidative damage to DNA and mitochondria. Selenium-mediated antioxidant enzymes (e.g., GPx and TrxR) activation enhance cellular metabolic activity [65].

Confocal microscopy images (Fig. 2C) showed the intracellular transport and distribution of SeYCQDs, visualized by their inherent blue fluorescence. As shown in Fig. 2C, both bEnd.3 and MC3T3-E1 cells efficiently internalized SeYCQDs within 24 h, with no morphological alterations observed in bright-field (BF) images, confirming minimal acute toxicity. The quantitative of fluorescence intensity profiles (Fig. 2D–E) showed that SeYCQDs were mainly concentrated around the nucleus both of bEnd.3 and MC3T3-E1 cells SeYCQDs predominantly localized perinuclearly in both cell types, which was highly coincident with the position of the nucleus, indicating that SeYCQDs may exert their biological effects by interacting with the nucleus.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Impact of SeYCQDs on cellular behavior under diabetic conditions
ce:section.ce:para: EdU staining assays revealed a 55–60 % suppression of proliferation in both bEnd.3 endothelial cells and MC3T3-E1 osteoprogenitors under diabetic conditions compared to normal conditions (Fig. 3 A). While NAC (N-acetylcysteine), a classic ROS scavenger [66], partially restored proliferation (∼10 % recovery), SeYCQDs exhibited superior efficacy. Quantitative analysis demonstrated that SeYCQDs elevated EdU-positive cell populations by 1.5-fold (bEnd.3) and 2.3-fold (MC3T3-E1) compared to the DM group (p < 0.0001; Fig. 3B–C). Diabetic conditions induced profound cytoskeletal disorganization and 50–60 % reduction in adhesion capacity across both cell types (Fig. 3D). DM-treated cells exhibited fragmented actin filaments and diminished vinculin expression (a key focal adhesion marker [67]). NAC partially stabilized actin architecture (30–40 % improvement), whereas SeYCQDs achieved over 80 % restoration of cytoskeletal integrity and vinculin localization. Quantitatively, SeYCQDs upregulated vinculin levels by 2.1-fold (bEnd.3) and 2.5-fold (MC3T3-E1) compared to the DM group (p < 0.0001; Fig. 3E–F), outperforming NAC by 1.5–1.7-fold (p < 0.05). This suggests that SeYCQDs not only neutralize ROS but also actively repair redox-sensitive structural proteins critical for cell-matrix interactions.

The superior performance of SeYCQDs may be attributed to their unique selenium-based antioxidant mechanisms. In contrast to NAC, which primarily exerts its effects by directly scavenging ROS (such as hydroxyl radicals and superoxide anions) and promoting glutathione (GSH) synthesis, SeYCQDs not only scavenge ROS directly but may also activate the Nrf2 signaling pathway [59,62]. This activation could induce the expression of endogenous antioxidant enzymes, such as superoxide dismutase (SOD), glutathione peroxidase (GPx), and catalase (CAT), providing more sustained protection. Furthermore, the exceptional antioxidant performance of SeYCQDs is closely linked to the presence of selenium, a critical cofactor for GPx, which efficiently neutralizes peroxides such as H2O2 and lipid peroxides [65]. These properties make SeYCQDs particularly effective in mitigating oxidative stress over the long term. Moreover, the nuclear-peripheral localization of SeYCQDs (Fig. 2) may offer additional protection to DNA and nuclear structures, a feature not observed with NAC. These findings indicate that SeYCQDs possess not only potent antioxidant capabilities but also unique selenium-driven mechanisms and dual functionalities, offering promising potential for the treatment of diabetes-related complications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Osteogenic activity of SeYCQDs in diabetic microenvironments
ce:section.ce:para: Under diabetic conditions, qPCR analysis revealed a 60–70 % suppression of key osteogenic markers compared to NM group, including Runx2 (p < 0.001), Osterix (Osx) (p < 0.0001), Alp (p < 0.01), Bglap (osteocalcin, p < 0.001), and Opn (p < 0.05) (Fig. 4 A). Strikingly, Col1—a critical collagen synthesis gene—remained unaffected (p > 0.05), suggesting that hyperglycemia may be selectively disrupts osteoblast differentiation rather than matrix production. SeYCQDs reversed this suppression, restoring Runx2, Osterix, Alp, Bglap, and Opn to NM-equivalent or higher levels (p < 0.05 to p < 0.0001). Notably, Col1 expression was upregulated by 1.8-fold compared to DM (p < 0.0001) and 1.3-fold versus NM (p < 0.05), indicating a unique capacity to enhance collagen synthesis beyond physiological baselines. This dual action—rescuing differentiation and amplifying matrix production—positions SeYCQDs as a multifunctional osteoanabolic agent. Immunofluorescence staining and quantitative analysis revealed that SeYCQDs intervention induced a 2.4-fold increase in Runx2 nuclear translocation compared to the DM group (p < 0.01; Fig. 4B–C), consistent with qPCR results demonstrating significant upregulation of Runx2 expression. The coordinated upregulation of Runx2 (the master regulator of osteogenic differentiation) and Osx indicates that SeYCQDs reprogram osteogenic differentiation through activation of the canonical Runx2/Osx signaling axis, rather than bypassing Runx2-dependent pathways. This dual transcriptional regulatory mechanism synergistically drives downstream effector gene expression (e.g., Col1a1, Bglap), thereby overcoming diabetes-associated osteogenic dysfunction.

Alkaline phosphatase (ALP) activity, a hallmark of early osteogenic differentiation, was reduced by 70 % in DM compared to NM (p < 0.0001;Fig. 4D–E). SeYCQDs restored ALP levels to 85 % of NM (p < 0.0001). This rapid rescue aligns with Alp gene upregulation, confirming functional translation of transcriptional changes, which indicates that SeYCQDs mitigate the inhibitory effects of diabetic conditions on early osteoblast differentiation. Matrix mineralization, assessed via Alizarin Red S (ARS) staining (Fig. 4F–G), exhibited 80 % impairment under DM. SeYCQDs restored ARS levels to over 80 % of NM (p < 0.0001). This phenomenon may be attributed to the fact that the increased Col1 expression by SeYCQDs provides a good extracellular matrix for cells. In addition, SeYCQDs may act as nano-scale scaffolds to promote calcium and phosphorus crystallization through surface charge interaction.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Angiogenic activity of SeYCQDs in vitro
ce:section.ce:para: Given the critical role of angiogenesis in supporting osteogenesis, we further investigated the angiogenic potential of SeYCQDs using a co-culture system of osteoblasts (MC3T3-E1) and endothelial cells (bEnd.3). Angiogenesis not only provides essential nutrients and oxygen for bone formation but also facilitates the recruitment of osteogenic precursors and the coupling of vascularization with bone remodeling. Therefore, assessing the angiogenic activity of SeYCQDs under diabetic conditions is crucial to understanding their role in promoting bone regeneration.

The migratory capacity of endothelial cells, a key step in angiogenesis, was evaluated using a wound healing assay. The result revealed severe impairment of bEnd.3 cell migration under diabetic conditions (DM), with delayed wound closure at both 6 h (1-fold larger wound area vs. NM group, p < 0.01) and 20 h (over 10-fold larger, p < 0.0001) (Fig. 5 A–C). SeYCQDs treatment significantly restored migration capacity, achieving 95 ± 1.2 % wound closure at 20 h (vs. DM: 79.9 ± 3.9 %, p < 0.0001). The improvement in migration indicates that SeYCQDs counteract the inhibitory effects of diabetic conditions on endothelial cells. This is likely mediated by their antioxidant properties, which alleviate oxidative stress-induced impairments in cellular motility. Enhanced endothelial migration lays the groundwork for effective angiogenesis [68], a process essential for bone regeneration.

To assess the angiogenic capacity of endothelial cells, a tubule formation assay was performed. Under diabetic conditions, the formation of capillary-like structures by bEnd.3 cells was severely disrupted, as evidenced by a marked reduction in the number of junctions and total vessel length compared to the NM group (Fig. 5D). SeYCQDs treatment significantly enhanced tubule formation, restoring the number of junctions (p < 0.05) and total vessel length (p < 0.01, Fig. 5E–F). These findings demonstrate that SeYCQDs promote endothelial cell angiogenesis under diabetic conditions, overcoming the inhibitory effects of hyperglycemia-induced oxidative stress.

Angiogenesis plays a critical role in providing oxygen and nutrients to bone-forming cells and is thus a crucial component of effective bone regeneration. The ability of SeYCQDs to enhance tubule formation suggests their potential to support vascular remodeling, a key requirement for coupling angiogenesis with osteogenesis. To further elucidate the effects of SeYCQDs on angiogenesis-osteogenesis coupling, a co-culture system of endothelial cells (bEnd.3) and osteoblasts (MC3T3-E1) was used to analyze the expression of key genes related to both processes (Fig. S3). Under diabetic conditions, the expression of angiogenic genes, including Vegf, Pdgfa, and Fgf1, was significantly downregulated compared to the NM group (Fig. 5G–I). SeYCQDs treatment markedly restored the expression of these genes, with levels significantly higher than those in the DM group (p < 0.001 to p < 0.0001) and approaching or exceeding those in the NM group. Additionally, genes involved in angiogenesis-osteogenesis coupling, such as Noggin, Bmp2, and Wnt3a, followed a similar trend (Fig. 5J–L). SeYCQDs significantly upregulated their expression compared to the DM group (p < 0.05 to p < 0.0001). Notably, Bmp2 and Wnt3a are key regulators of bone formation [69], and their restoration under SeYCQDs treatment highlights the ability of SeYCQDs to promote the interplay between vascularization and osteogenesis. These results suggest that SeYCQDs not only enhance angiogenesis but also strengthen the molecular signals required for angiogenesis-osteogenesis coupling, an essential mechanism for coordinated bone regeneration.

The ability of SeYCQDs to upregulate both angiogenic and coupling-related genes is likely driven by their dual antioxidant and pro-angiogenic properties. By reducing oxidative stress, SeYCQDs may restore endothelial and osteoblastic signaling pathways that are disrupted under diabetic conditions. Furthermore, the enhanced expression of Vegf and Bmp2 suggests that SeYCQDs actively support cross-talk between endothelial cells and osteoblasts, providing a molecular basis for improved bone regeneration in diabetes-induced bone defects. The results demonstrated that SeYCQDs effectively promote angiogenesis through enhanced endothelial cell migration and tubule formation, as well as through upregulation of angiogenesis-related genes. Moreover, SeYCQDs facilitate angiogenesis-osteogenesis coupling by restoring the expression of key coupling genes, such as Bmp2 and Wnt3a, in a co-culture system. These findings align with earlier results on the osteogenic activity of SeYCQDs (Fig. 4), emphasizing their comprehensive role in enhancing both vascular and bone regeneration under diabetic conditions. By addressing the interconnected processes of angiogenesis and osteogenesis, SeYCQDs show promise as a therapeutic agent for treating diabetes-related bone disorders.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Transcriptomic analysis of SeYCQDs under diabetic conditions
ce:section.ce:para: To investigate the molecular mechanisms underlying the therapeutic effects of SeYCQDs on endothelial cells under diabetic conditions, we performed transcriptomic analysis and key experimental validations (Fig. S4). We first confirmed the reliability of the data set by using PCA (Fig. S5) and sample correlation matrix (Fig. S6) for quality control. A total of 101 differentially expressed genes (DEGs) were identified in the DM_SeYCQD group, including 78 up-regulated and 23 down-regulated genes compared to the DM group (Fig. S7).

The DEG heatmap revealed significant differences in transcriptome profiles between the two groups, reflecting the broad regulatory effects of SeYCQDs (Fig. S8). Gene Ontology(GO)enrichment analysis revealed significant changes in biological processes (BP), cellular components (CC), and molecular functions (MF) associated with oxidative stress, adhesion, extracellular matrix organization, and antioxidant activity (Fig. 6 A–C). Among these, processes such as "cellular response to oxidative stress," "collagen-containing extracellular matrix," and "antioxidant activity" were particularly enriched (Fig. 6D). Kyoto Encyclopedia of Genes and Genomes (KEGG) pathway analysis further identified critical pathways, including ECM-receptor interaction, NF-κB signaling, and apoptosis pathways (Fig. 6E). These results indicate that SeYCQDs modulate multiple pathways essential for vascular formation, adhesion, and antioxidant defenses under diabetic conditions. Gene Set Enrichment Analysis (GSEA) confirmed significant enrichment of genes in pathways related to apoptosis (Fig. 6F) and intrinsic apoptotic signaling in response to oxidative stress (Fig. 6G), highlighting the dual antioxidant and anti-inflammatory mechanisms of SeYCQDs. Heatmaps of DEGs demonstrated that SeYCQDs treatment significantly upregulated genes involved in vascular formation (Vegfd, Fbln2), adhesion and migration (Arhgap20, Mmp3, Bgn, Srpx2), and antioxidant/inflammation-related responses (Sod3, Nqo1, Gsta3, Ccl2, Il34) compared to the DM group (Fig. 6H–J). The protein-protein interaction (PPI) network (Fig. 6K) further emphasized the connectivity and functional importance of these genes in restoring vascular and oxidative balance.

qPCR validation confirmed the transcriptomic results. Vascular formation-related genes (Vegfd, Fbln2) were significantly upregulated in the DM_SeYCQD group compared to the DM group (Fig. 6M), consistent with SeYCQDs' ability to promote angiogenesis. Similarly, antioxidant-related genes (Sod3, Nqo1, Gsta3) showed significant increases in expression under SeYCQD treatment, highlighting its capacity to mitigate oxidative stress (Fig. 6N). Furthermore, inflammation-related genes (Ccl2, Cxcl2, Il34) were markedly regulated, indicating SeYCQDs' role in suppressing inflammatory responses (Fig. 6O).

These findings underscore the multifaceted regulatory effects of SeYCQDs under diabetic conditions. By modulating key genes and pathways involved in vascular formation, adhesion, and antioxidant/inflammatory responses, SeYCQDs effectively address the multifactorial nature of diabetes-induced dysfunction. The upregulation of Vegfd and Fbln2 supports angiogenesis, while the enhancement of Sod3, Nqo1, and Gsta3 provides robust antioxidant protection. Moreover, the regulation of inflammatory genes such as Ccl2 and Il34 highlights SeYCQDs' anti-inflammatory potential. Together, these results provide a comprehensive molecular basis for the therapeutic efficacy of SeYCQDs in promoting vascular repair and mitigating oxidative and inflammatory damage.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Validation of the antioxidant and cytoprotective effects of SeYCQDs under diabetic conditions
ce:section.ce:para: To systematically validate the antioxidative and cytoprotective properties of SeYCQDs under diabetic stress, we employed a multi-layered analytical approach encompassing oxidative stress biomarkers, antioxidant gene networks, mitochondrial homeostasis, and apoptosis regulation. The DCFH-DA fluorescence quantification revealed a 3.3-fold increase in intracellular ROS levels in the DM group compared to NM controls (P < 0.0001, Fig. 7 A–B), confirming hyperglycemia-induced oxidative overload. SeYCQDs treatment effectively normalized ROS production, achieving 55 % reduction versus the DM group (P < 0.0001). This scavenging capacity was further corroborated by lipid peroxidation markers: MDA levels surged 3.7-fold under diabetic conditions (P < 0.0001 vs. NM), while SeYCQDs restored MDA to NM-equivalent levels (P < 0.0001, Fig. 7C). Concurrently, the DM group exhibited 8.5 % depletion of reduced glutathione (GSH) compared to NM (P < 0.01), whereas SeYCQDs not only reversed GSH depletion but elevated its concentration 1.5-fold above NM levels (P < 0.0001, Fig. 7D), demonstrating superior redox homeostasis modulation.

qPCR profiling uncovered SeYCQDs' transcriptional regulation of enzymatic antioxidant defenses: Diabetic conditions suppressed Gpx4 expression by 77 % (P < 0.01 vs. NM), while SeYCQDs upregulated it 3.2-fold (P < 0.01 vs. DM), critical for neutralizing lipid hydroperoxides (Fig. 7E). SOD Isoform Synergy: SOD1, SOD2, and SOD3 expressions were collectively enhanced 1.5–4-fold by SeYCQDs (P < 0.0001 vs. DM) (Fig. 7F). CAT expression increased 8.9-fold with SeYCQDs (P < 0.01 vs. DM), augmenting H2O2 detoxification (Fig. 7G). SIRT3—a master regulator of mitochondrial antioxidant responses [70]—was upregulated 3.3-fold (P < 0.0001 vs. DM) (Fig. 7H), suggesting an additional layer of mitochondrial antioxidant protection. In addition to antioxidant enzymes, Nrf2 mRNA expression itself was also significantly altered by treatment. Compared to the NM group, DM condition downregulated Nrf2 expression (p < 0.0001), while SeYCQDs treatment restored and significantly increased Nrf2 expression by 3.2-fold over the DM group (p < 0.0001, Fig. 7I). This transcriptional upregulation, together with enhanced expression of its downstream targets (Gpx4, Sod3, Nqo1, SIRT3), provides strong evidence for Nrf2/ARE signaling axis activation.

JC-1 staining quantified mitochondrial membrane potential (ΔΨm) collapse under diabetic stress: the DM group showed 1.4-fold more cells with depolarized mitochondria than NM (P < 0.0001, Fig. 7J). SeYCQDs reduced depolarized cells by 31 % (P < 0.0001 vs. DM), restoring ΔΨm to 99 % of NM levels (Fig. 7K). This mitochondrial protection aligns with SIRT3-mediated enhancement of electron transport chain efficiency and reduced proton leakage [71]. Annexin V/PI dual staining revealed that hyperglycemia-triggered apoptosis (DM: 18.73 ± 1.33 % early apoptotic cells) was attenuated to 7.09 ± 0.65 % by SeYCQDs (P < 0.0001 vs. DM), comparable to NM controls (3.96 ± 0.38 %) (Fig. 7L–M). The anti-apoptotic efficacy correlated strongly with ROS reduction and ΔΨm recovery, confirming SeYCQDs disrupt the ROS-mitochondria-apoptosis axis. Although organelle-specific co-localization assays were not performed in the present study, the perinuclear fluorescence distribution observed in confocal imaging (Fig. 2C–E), together with the significant restoration of mitochondrial membrane potential (Fig. 7J–K), supports mitochondrial accumulation of SeYCQDs. Previous reports have demonstrated that selenium-based nanozymes preferentially localize to mitochondria due to their electrochemical and redox interactions with the organelle microenvironment [72–74].

These findings establish SeYCQDs as multi-tiered redox modulators: Primary Defense: Direct ROS neutralization via Se-mediated electron transfer. Secondary Defense: Transcriptional activation of SODs, CAT, and Gpx4 through putative Nrf2/ARE pathway engagement. Mitochondrial Optimization: SIRT3-driven enhancement anti-apoptotic family regulation. The coordinated antioxidant-cytoprotective effects substantiate SeYCQDs' therapeutic superiority over conventional selenium formulations, particularly in counteracting diabetes-associated oxidative insults. These results mechanistically support the enhanced angiogenesis and osteogenesis observed in prior experiments (Figs. 5–6), confirming SeYCQDs' capacity to break the vicious cycle of oxidative damage-impaired regeneration in diabetic bone healing.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Validation of SeYCQDs' effects on angiogenesis-osteogenesis coupling in vivo
ce:section.ce:para:
To investigate the therapeutic effects of SeYCQDs on angiogenesis and osteogenesis under diabetic conditions, a T1D mouse model was established and treated with SeYCQDs. Fig. 8 A illustrates the experimental timeline, including drug administration and sample analysis at 1, 7, 14, and 28 days post-treatment. Immunofluorescence imaging demonstrated severe type H vessel (CD31
+
EMCN
+ ) depletion in diabetic bone, with the DM group exhibiting only 43.5 % of NM group vessel density (P < 0.0001, Fig. 8B). SeYCQDs intervention reversed this deficit, achieving 90 % recovery of type H vessel volume versus DM (P < 0.0001, Fig. 8C). Notably, regenerated vessels exhibited peri-trabecular spatial patterning, with 84 % of CD31
+
EMCN
+ structures localized osteoid surfaces (vs. 32 % in DM), recapitulating the NM group's physiological vascular-bone interface (Fig. 8B, Surface rendering). Quantification of Osx
+ osteoprogenitors revealed a 1.5-fold increase in active osteoblast volume in the DM_SeYCQD group compared to DM (P < 0.0001, Fig. 8D). Spatial mapping identified 72 % of Osx
+ cells within type H vessels—a 3.1-fold enhancement over DM (P < 0.001)—confirming reestablished vascular-osteogenic niche interactions.

To further explore the molecular mechanisms underlying SeYCQDs’ effects on angiogenesis-osteogenesis coupling, we analyzed the expression of key coupling-related genes. SeYCQDs significantly upregulated angiogenic and osteogenic coupling genes, including Noggin (2.1-fold), Vegf (2.7-fold), Fgf1 (2.5-fold), Igfb1 (4.2-fold), Pdgfa (1.6-fold), Bmp2 (2.3-fold), and Wnt3a (2.1-fold), compared to the DM group (P < 0.01 to P < 0.0001) (Fig. 8E). These results suggest that SeYCQDs enhance vascular-osteogenic interactions by promoting the expression of key signaling molecules involved in the coupling process. Additionally, osteogenesis-specific gene expression was analyzed to validate the effects of SeYCQDs on bone formation. SeYCQDs significantly increased the expression of key osteogenic markers such as Osx (1.8-fold), Runx2(2.1-fold), Alp (1.6-fold), Bglap (2.5-fold), Opn (2.8-fold) and Col1 (2.8-fold) compared to the DM group (P < 0.001 to P < 0.0001) (Fig. 8F). These findings seggests the pro-osteogenic effects of SeYCQDs under diabetic conditions.

The results from Fig. 8 provide strong evidence for the therapeutic potential of SeYCQDs in promoting angiogenesis and osteogenesis under diabetic conditions. The recovery of type H vessels, a critical subtype of vasculature involved in angiogenesis-osteogenesis coupling, underscores SeYCQDs' ability to restore vascular function and its interaction with osteoblasts. The spatial colocalization of CD31
+
EMCN
+ vessels with Osx
+ osteoblasts high lights the coordinated recovery of vascular and bone-forming elements. At the molecular level, the upregulation of angiogenic-osteogenic coupling genes (e.g., Vegf, Bmp2, Wnt3a) further supports the hypothesis that SeYCQDs enhance the crosstalk between endothelial cells and osteoblasts. This is consistent with previous findings in Fig. 5, where SeYCQDs were shown to improve endothelial cell function, and in Figs. 6 and 7, which demonstrated their antioxidant and cytoprotective effects. Fig. 8 demonstrates that SeYCQDs effectively promote angiogenesis and osteogenesis in vivo by restoring type H vessel formation, enhancing osteoblast function, and upregulating key coupling-related signaling pathways. These findings, combined with their molecular and cellular effects shown in previous figures, highlight the therapeutic potential of SeYCQDs for treating diabetic complications involving impaired vascular and bone regeneration.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effects of SeYCQDs on new bone formation under diabetic conditions in vivo
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate the therapeutic effects of SeYCQDs on bone regeneration in vivo, a T1D mouse model was analyzed 28 days post-treatment (Fig. 9 A). MicroCT analysis revealed significant trabecular bone loss in the DM group compared to the NM group (Fig. 9B). Bone Mass Metrics: The DM group exhibited 23 % reduction in BMD (P < 0.001 vs. NM) and 79 % loss in BV/TV (P < 0.001), consistent with severe osteopenia (Fig. 9C–D). SeYCQDs restored BMD to 84 % of NM levels (P < 0.01 vs. DM) and BV/TV to 44 % (P < 0.05). Structural Parameters: Tb.Th: Reduced by 40 % in DM (P < 0.0001, Fig. 9E). Tb.N: Reduced by 67 % in DM (P < 0.001) but recovered to 65 % of NM density with SeYCQDs (P < 0.01, Fig. 9F). Tb.Sp: Expanded to 486.8 U in DM (1.3-fold vs. NM, P < 0.05), narrowed to 361.2 U with SeYCQDs (P < 0.01, Fig. 9G). These results suggest that SeYCQDs effectively mitigate trabecular bone loss and improve bone microstructure under diabetic conditions. Cortical bone microstructural parameters were also assessed via Micro-CT (Fig. 9H–M). Unlike the trabecular region, cortical bone exhibited minimal structural changes across different groups. cTMD, BS/BV, Ct.Th, MMI, BMA and BAr: The DM group showed a significant decrease compared to NM group (P > 0.05, Fig. 9H–J, L–M), which was no significant increase by SeYCQDs (P > 0.05 vs. DM). Porosity (Po): The DM group showed a significant increase in cortical porosity (P < 0.0001 vs. NM, Fig. 9K), which was no significant reduced by SeYCQDs (P > 0.05 vs. DM). These results suggest that while SeYCQDs effectively restore trabecular bone mass and microstructure, their effects on cortical bone are primarily limited to reducing porosity, with minimal impact on overall cortical bone remodeling.

Histological analysis further supported these findings. Masson's trichrome staining demonstrated disrupted trabecular bone structure and reduced collagen deposition in the DM group compared to the NM group. In contrast, the DM_SeYCQD group exhibited significantly improved trabecular bone collagen deposition and structure (Fig. 9N). Quantitative analysis showed a significant increase in the volume fraction of new bone in the DM_SeYCQD group compared to the DM group (P < 0.01) (Fig. 9O). Von Kossa (VG) staining revealed significantly reduced trabecular mineralization in the DM group compared to the NM group. SeYCQDs significantly improved trabecular mineralization, as evidenced by the restoration of mineralized bone volume in the DM_SeYCQD group compared to the DM group (P < 0.01) (Fig. 9P). Quantitative analysis confirmed this result, with a significant increase in the percentage of mineralized bone in the DM_SeYCQD group (P < 0.01) (Fig. 9Q).

These demonstrate the ability of SeYCQDs to promote trabecular bone formation under diabetic conditions. The restoration of trabecular bone microarchitecture, including increased BMD, BV/TV, Tb.N, and Tb.Th, highlights the potential of SeYCQDs in reversing diabetic bone loss. These findings align with earlier results (e.g., Fig. 8), where SeYCQDs were shown to enhance angiogenesis and vascular-osteogenic coupling, further supporting the hypothesis that improved vascularization facilitates trabecular bone regeneration. Histological analyses of Masson's trichrome and VG staining provide further evidence of SeYCQDs' therapeutic effects, demonstrating enhanced collagen deposition and mineralization in trabecular regions. The improved trabecular bone matrix and mineral content reflect the combined effects of SeYCQDs on osteogenesis and oxidative stress reduction, as shown in Fig. 7. The focus on trabecular bone may also reflect its greater sensitivity to vascular and osteogenic interventions compared to cortical bone, as trabecular bone turnover is more metabolically active and directly dependent on angiogenesis. These results highlight the unique potential of SeYCQDs to target the angiogenic and osteogenic processes that are critical for trabecular bone regeneration.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Biocompatibility assessment of SeYCQDs in vivo
ce:section.ce:para: Two months after the SeYCQDs treatment, we conducted histopathological analysis of major organs and hematological assessment on diabetic mice. The H&E staining results showed that no histopathological abnormalities were observed in the heart, liver, spleen, lung, and kidney in the SeYCQD group compared with the control group (Fig. 10 A). This indicates that SeYCQDs have excellent biocompatibility and no obvious organ toxicity under diabetic conditions.

The Hematological parameters results showed that for the white blood cell profile (Fig. 10B), the WBC count in the SeYCQD group was slightly increased compared with the vehicle group (P < 0.05), while the counts of LYM, MON, and NEU remained unchanged (P > 0.05). For the erythrocyte indices (Fig. 10C), there were no significant differences in the RBC count, HGB level, MCH, or MCV between the SeYCQD group and the vehicle group (P > 0.05), indicating that SeYCQD does not affect erythropoiesis. For the hemoglobin concentration (Fig. 10D): MCHC exhibited a slight decrease (P < 0.01), while HCT remained stable (P > 0.05). Regarding the platelet parameters (Fig. 10E), the PLT count in the SeYCQD group was significantly lower than that in the vehicle group (P < 0.001), but the MPV, PDW, and PCT remained unchanged (P > 0.05), indicating that SeYCQD does not disrupt the overall platelet function. These results indicate that SeYCQD shows good in vivo biocompatibility, with no evidence of organ toxicity or significant hematological disorders. Although slight changes in white blood cell and platelet counts were observed, they are still within the physiological range, indicating no significant adverse effects. These findings emphasize that SeYCQDs are a safe nanomaterial that can be used in the treatment of diabetic bone regeneration.

The in vitro biocompatibility of SeYCQDs (Fig. 2A–B), together with the absence of histological abnormalities in major organs (Fig. 10), confirms the biosafety of the nanozyme across both cellular and systemic levels. These results collectively reinforce the translational promise of SeYCQDs for safe clinical use.

This study innovatively integrates yeast metabolic engineering with biomimetic nanozyme design to develop selenium-enriched yeast-derived carbon quantum dots (SeYCQDs), achieving dual therapeutic objectives of ROS scavenging and vascular-bone metabolic axis reconstruction. SeYCQDs exhibit robust antioxidant activity by enhancing the expression of endogenous antioxidant enzymes, effectively mitigating oxidative stress, restoring diabetes-impaired type H vessel functionality, and promoting angiogenesis-osteogenesis coupling. Both in vitro and in vivo experiments validate their remarkable efficacy in enhancing endothelial cell proliferation, migration, tube formation, as well as osteoblast differentiation and mineralization. Furthermore, SeYCQDs exert therapeutic effects by modulating key molecular pathways associated with oxidative stress, inflammation, and angiogenesis. This "one-material-dual-target" strategy not only provides a precision therapeutic approach for DBD but also pioneers novel pathways for nanozyme applications in metabolic disorders. Future investigations will focus on exploring the clinical translation potential of SeYCQDs and their applicability in other bone-related diseases.
